model_narrative: We plan electricity capacity expansion to meet a known set of demands. First we decide how much generation capacity to install for each type of power plant. Then we decide how much each plant type produces in each operating mode. We track the total cost, which combines the upfront investment cost of installing capacity and the operating cost of running the plants. The objective is to make the total cost as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the types of power plant whose capacity can be installed

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the operating modes in which installed plants can run

params.name: c
params.doc: investment cost to install one unit of capacity of each plant type, in cost units per unit of capacity

params.name: d
params.doc: the energy demand of each operating mode that must be met, in energy units

params.name: f
params.doc: operating cost to run a plant type in an operating mode, in cost units per unit of energy produced

params.name: m
params.doc: the minimum total installed capacity that must be in place across all plant types, in units of capacity

params.name: b
params.doc: the budget limit available for capacity investment, in cost units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the amount of capacity installed for each plant type, in units of capacity

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the operating level, that is the energy produced by each plant type when run in each operating mode, in energy units

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total cost, combining investment cost and operating cost, in cost units

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total cost brings together the cost of building capacity and the cost of running the plants. Take the investment cost of the capacity installed for every plant type, and add the operating cost of every plant type running in every operating mode. Set the total cost equal to the sum of these two pieces.

constraints.name: x[i]
constraints.intent: There is a floor on how much generation capacity must be in place. The capacity installed across all plant types together must be at least the required minimum total capacity.

constraints.name: c[i]
constraints.intent: Spending on capacity is limited by an available budget. The total investment cost of the capacity installed across all plant types must not exceed the budget.

constraints.name: y[i,
constraints.intent: A plant can only produce up to the capacity that has been installed for it. For each plant type, the total energy it produces summed over all operating modes must not exceed the capacity installed for that plant type.

constraints.name: y[i,
constraints.intent: Each operating mode has a demand that has to be served. For each operating mode, the total energy produced in that mode summed over all plant types must be at least the demand of that mode.

query: There's a proposal on the table to firm up the dispatchable backbone - the two units that actually carry load day to day are plant-1 and plant-3, and reliability wants their combined installed capacity to land at no less than seven units between them. We're evaluating that floor now; what does the optimizer return for the build mix and total cost?

query: Plant-4 is little more than idle iron in this plan - four units installed and it barely dispatches - yet the flexible plant-1 we actually rely on is built thinner than that. Shouldn't plant-1's installed capacity stand at least half again above plant-4's, so the workhorse outweighs the parked asset? What's keeping the model from tilting the build that way?

query: The build keeps leaning on one big slug of capacity at a single plant instead of spreading the iron around. For siting and outage resilience, shouldn't no individual plant type be allowed to carry more than three and a half units on its own, so the fleet stays spread out? What's keeping the model from holding every plant under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.per_plant_ceiling = Constraint(model.i, rule=lambda mm, i: mm.x[i] <= 3.5)
